fuzzy_prd:
We need a small library that lets numerical code describe the shape of a multi-dimensional array and then translate any multi-index into a flat memory offset, so engineers never have to hand-derive stride formulas again. The library should support three storage conventions: the natural row-major convention where the last dimension changes fastest (used by C arrays), the column-major convention where the first dimension changes fastest (used by Fortran), and a custom-stride mode where the caller supplies the step size for each dimension directly (useful for non-contiguous views, padded buffers, or broadcast layouts). On top of that, there should be a shape descriptor type that can express a mix of dimensions fixed at compile time and dimensions that are only known at runtime; the descriptor should be queryable for its total rank, how many of its dimensions are runtime-sized, the compile-time pattern with runtime slots shown as a placeholder, and the fully resolved sizes after the runtime values are substituted in. For all three mapping types the library should report the per-dimension strides, the minimum number of storage slots the mapping spans, whether the mapping covers storage without any gaps, and the single linear offset for a given multi-index. The custom-stride mapping should be flagged as non-contiguous regardless of the actual numeric values supplied, because contiguity cannot be assumed for caller-provided strides. Zero strides are allowed in the custom mode — a mapping with all-zero strides collapses every multi-index onto offset zero and spans only a single element.

One small follow-up from the questions that came in: for the mixed static/runtime shape descriptor, runtime-sized axes are represented by the string 'dyn' in the static_extents output and in the input 'static' array, while fixed axes show their integer size. The output should stay exactly in the form 'static_extents=[<comma-separated values>]' and 'extents=[<comma-separated resolved sizes>]' using square brackets.

Also, to make the mapping behavior totally explicit, layout_stride always reports 'is_exhaustive=false' regardless of the actual stride values supplied, and layout_right and layout_left always report 'is_exhaustive=true'. Here, exhaustiveness is just whether the mapping covers storage contiguously with no gaps. For span calculations, layout_stride uses required_span_size = 1 + sum over each axis of (size-1) * |stride|, and when all strides are zero, required_span_size = 1 since only one element is spanned. For layout_right and layout_left, required_span_size = product of all sizes.

And one naming/detail note for the adapter layer: the layout_stride mapping in the adapter (op='layout_stride') corresponds to std::experimental::layout_stride in the C++ mdspan proposal, and callers supply explicit strides as a separate list alongside extents.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: struct Extents { int rank(); int rank_dynamic(); vector<int> static_extents(); vector<int> extents(); }; struct LayoutRight { vector<int> strides(); int required_span_size(); bool is_exhaustive(); int operator()(vector<int> index); }; struct LayoutLeft { ... }; struct LayoutStride { ... };

trigger_keywords: what is the output format for strides
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: All list-valued fields (strides, extents, static_extents) are printed as comma-separated values inside square brackets with no spaces: e.g., 'strides=[20,5,1]', 'extents=[3,4,5]'. Each fact is on its own line as 'key=value'.

trigger_keywords: how are row major strides derived
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: layout_right (row-major): last axis stride=1, each earlier axis stride = product of all sizes to its right. layout_left (column-major): first axis stride=1, each later axis stride = product of all sizes to its left. Offset = sum of index[i] * stride[i] for all axes.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: dimensions fixed at compile time and dimensions only known at runtime
context_pointers.actual_meaning: In the adapter input, each entry in the 'static' array is either an integer (compile-time fixed) or the string 'dyn' (runtime-sized). The 'dynamic' array provides the concrete values for 'dyn' slots in axis order.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific information about that constraint. Please refer to the project PRD and the golden implementation under kokkos__mdspan/golden/include/ for the authoritative specification.
